g.data-narration: Thread safety ensures code works correctly when accessed by multiple threads simultaneously in parallel computing.
Thread safety ensures code works correctly when accessed by multiple threads executing at the same time.
Thread safety prevents race conditions, data corruption, and undefined behavior in concurrent programs.
The increment operation count++ is NOT thread-safe and represents a race condition waiting to happen.
A race condition occurs when multiple threads access shared data with at least one thread writing, causing lost updates.
g.text: RACE CONDITION: Multiple threads access shared data, at least one writes

g.data-narration: Count++ is not atomic - it performs three separate operations: read, increment, and write, creating a race window.
g.text: count++ is NOT Atomic - Three Steps
thread can also READ the OLD value!

g.data-narration: A critical section is code that accesses shared resources and must execute by only one thread at a time using synchronization.
g.text: CRITICAL SECTION: Code that accesses shared resources must execute by only ONE thread at a time

g.data-narration: Four main synchronization solutions exist: mutex locks, atomic operations, reduction, and thread-local storage.
OpenMP critical directive ensures only one thread executes the code block at a time using mutual exclusion.
OpenMP atomic directive provides hardware-level atomic operations that are faster than critical for simple operations.
OpenMP reduction clause gives each thread a private copy, then combines them at the end, requiring no locks.
Deadlock occurs when threads wait indefinitely for each other. Prevent by acquiring locks in the same order.
g.text: DEADLOCK: Two or more threads wait indefinitely for each other

g.data-narration: Thread-local storage gives each thread its own copy of a variable, eliminating sharing and race conditions.
g.text: Each thread has its OWN copy

g.data-narration: Best practices: identify shared variables, minimize critical sections, prefer reduction, avoid deadlock, use atomic for simple ops.
Key takeaways: thread safety ensures correct execution, count++ is not atomic, use critical atomic or reduction, avoid deadlock.

g.text: count++ is NOT atomic - has READ/MODIFY/WRITE
Use critical, atomic, or reduction for safety
Deadlock prevention: acquire locks in order

g.data-narration: Fixed thread-safe counter using atomic operation ensures correct increment without race conditions.
